title: Lesson 244
transcript: In this section, we're going to describe how we actually protect assets as they go through their life cycle. By this point, we've classified assets into their classification, which obviously portrays value, but how do we actually give them the level of protection that they require? The answer to this is simply baselines. Baselines, as you know, are minimum levels of security requirements and so if we have classification levels such as top secret, secret, confidential, All we need to do at this point is come up with minimum levels of security requirements for each of those. So we could have for top secret, we could say, all right, anything that is top secret requires two factor authentication, real time logging. 256-bit symmetric encryption and so for something that is secret we would have less security requirements and those are those baselines that we establish for every classification level that is part of our asset classification systems. The idea is as assets go through their life cycle whether they increase or decrease in classification doesn't matter because we already know what the minimum security requirements for each of those classifications actually are. So the owner always keeps track and keeps an eye on their assets and if they increase or decrease in classification we simply need to apply the controls that we have established as baselines for each of the classification levels. Now obviously as assets go through their life cycle they can be in different states and I'm sure you've heard of the different states for example that data can be in or information can be in. Typically these are the three states that data can be in. You've heard of this data at rest, data in motion which is also referred to as data in transit and some people would argue that there's a third state that data can be in and that is data in use. Now let's talk about data in use first of all very briefly. Data in use which means it's being looked at or processed is extremely challenging to protect because typically it needs to be in its native format in plain text for example. And so the only way that you can protect data in use is typically through what are called enclaves. And the idea of an enclave is simply a isolated area where that data is being processed. but nothing else can get to that area. So we use the concept of isolation that we've talked about before in this class where you've got an area of storage for example that no one else or nothing else can get to while that data is being processed. Now let's focus on the other two states. data in rest or data at rest and data in transit. Now if you think about what this means data at rest is simply data that's being stored for example in a file or in a database or and the way that we protect it is based on the risks that can impact that data. So if you think of a file that's stored on your laptop on the hard drive, you've got risks associated with confidentiality, integrity and availability and so how do we protect anything that has risks associated with confidentiality integrity and availability typically the methods that we use are access controls encryption and from the perspective of availability we add redundancy. So your typical controls that we apply to anything that is being stored at rest is what we would address from a security perspective. Now data in transit which is also referred to as data in motion simply means data that is moving typically across networks and protecting data that's moving in networks is particularly challenging as well but if you think about sending an email or some information across a network, the risks associated with this are actually the same as the ones that apply to a file or something that is being stored at rest. So if you think about an email message moving across a network, how do we protect that? Well the risks are the same. We've got risks associated with confidentiality. We've got risks associated with integrity. We've got risks associated with availability. So we protect the exact same way using good access controls We use encryption and we use redundancy to address the availability issue. Now if you think about data moving across networks, one of the best ways to protect that data is to use encryption. Now as far as network encryption methods, and those are the key words that you need to look for on the exam. Data network encryption methods, there are only two. We can use what are called end-to-end encryption, and link encryption. So there's only two ways to protect across a network and these are them. End-to-end encryption, link encryption. So let's look at each of these as you'll probably see some questions that relate to this. When you think about end-to-end encryption, it describes perfectly what we're doing here. We protect the data from end to end. So this is best for protecting data and its value. The way that it works is we encrypt right at the start and then the data will travel throughout each of the nodes of that network until it gets to its destination and that's where it's going to be decrypted. So plain text is only available at both ends, not in between. That protects data best. But what has to be visible in order for this to work because of the nature of our networks is the routing information has to be visible across each of the nodes of the network. So data is protected from end to end, but what has to be visible is the routing information and that's why we say end-to-end is best for protecting the data but the routing information has to be visible so we've got some animation here that you're going to see on the slide that describes perfectly how this works. And so you see that as that data travels throughout each of the nodes, it is protected because plain text is only available at both ends, protects data best. Now the other way to do things is what we call link encryption. And the reason it's called link encryption is because what we need to do here is we need to decrypt and re-encrypt the entire thing at every node along the way, at every link along the way. So the idea is, if I start the process, I'm going to encrypt the entire thing. And if you remember how we send units of information across the network, they're called packets or datagrams. But packets are really made up of two components. the header portion where the routing information is, and the data portion. So we encrypt everything, the header and the data portion. But then what happens is when the first node receives it, it will decrypt the information that it shares with the key that sent that information. It will look at the entire information, the header and the data, decide for example where it needs to go next, and then re-encrypt the entire thing, the header and the data, with the key that it shares with the next device along the way. So when that device receives it, it will decrypt the entire thing with the key that it shares with a device that just sent it to it. It will look at the routing information, decide where it needs to go next and then re-encrypt the entire thing with the key that that device shares with the next device along the way. The problem with this is that plain text is available at every node because every node needs to decrypt the entire information including the header and the data portion. So link encryption does not protect the data best because every link, every node along the way can potentially be a attack point. But maybe one of the advantages of link encryption might be that you can hide the routing information but only from this device to device or link to link so this does not protect data best but it allows us to hide the routing information so the question is why would you want to use this and the answer is if you need to hide the routing information between certain devices. It would be very difficult to achieve link encryption across a network that you don't control such as the internet but you could do something like this across your internal network if it was important to hide certain routing information between certain segments of your network. And so what you need to remember is that in end-to-end encryption protects data best. In link encryption, we can hide the routing information. Now an example of end-to-end encryption is a VPN solution. We all use those, but that's a perfect example of what we call end-to-end encryption. Link encryption and examples of those are very difficult to find but something that the industry has come up with to try and address the problems of link encryption which is what we're showing you here through the animation on this slide Because then not only is the routing information available at every node, but also the data is available at every node. So something that the industry has come up with is what we call onion networks. And if you can picture an onion in your mind, it's exactly what we're doing here. An onion has a whole bunch of layers, for example. And the idea here is that if I initiate the transmission of information, I need to do a lot of work ahead of time. to be able to figure out exactly how many nodes that information is gonna go through. And what I do is I build each layer up understanding how many nodes it needs to go through. The idea is as it travels through a network, each node will peel off the outer layer and only be able to see the routing information to the next And then that node, when it receives it, will peel off the next layer, look at the routing information that is only available for it to understand where it needs to go next. So the idea of onion networks is to really protect the data, but to also provide anonymity because each node along the way is not able to understand for example ultimately where that information is destined for and we also protect the information so that it's only available really at both ends. So it's almost like you know the perfect way to address the requirements of end-to-end and link without any of the problems that are associated. So we've got some animation here that I think describes this purpose of onion networks perfectly. And so you see that the device that originates this is going to have to do some work and understand, for example, how many nodes that information needs to go through, and we add that many layers of protection or encryption. And then as we send it to the next node, that node will only appeal off the outer layer, see the routing information and send it to the next device. Now that device will peel off the outer layer, see the routing information and forward it on to the next device until it gets to its destination. And that's where the data will be available. But the advantage of this is that devices along the way cannot see the data or ultimately where that data is destined for, providing not only protection for the data, but also anonymity. you
